FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=10,317, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years, Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization only, measured via single question about online harm/meanness on social media (Instagram, Snapchat); no separate phone/SM exposure variable measured. E6 applies: cyberbullying only, no separately-measured phone/SM variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cyberbullying is the dependent variable (outcome), not the exposure. No phone/SM variable reported in any association test.

FT_IC4.evidence: No phone/SM numeric reported; only ACE and cyberbullying associations presented.

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical analysis using logistic regression on longitudinal ABCD data (baseline to Year 2)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescence, 2023, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2; cyberbullying is outcome but no phone/SM variable is separately measured, so FT_EC7 determination is not applicable.
notes: Paper mentions screen time and digital media use in discussion but does not measure phone/SM as a separate variable in analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=9,443, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying only (victimization and perpetration). Paper adjusts for 'total recreational screen time' in models but reports no separately-extractable phone/SM-specific coefficient. E2/E3 rules: only composite screen time reported, no phone/SM-specific pathway extractable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cyberbullying is the predictor; sleep disturbance is the outcome. No phone/SM variable reported in any association test.

FT_IC4.evidence: No phone/SM numeric reported; screen time is covariate only with no separately reported coefficient.

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression on ABCD Year 2 data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2023, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2; no separately-measured phone/SM variable, so FT_EC7 is not applicable.
notes: Screen time is included as a covariate but not as the primary exposure variable; coefficients for screen time are reported in Model 3 but paper focuses on cyberbullying-sleep association.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=10,339, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific SM and phone variables: recreational screen time (general), television, YouTube videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat, browsing internet; also measures problematic use of video games, social media, and mobile phones via VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses linking sexual orientation (predictor) to recreational screen time and problematic use of video games, social media, and mobile phones (outcomes). Tests reported with confidence intervals.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β coefficients with 95% CI: sexual minority 3.72 (95% CI 2.96-4.47) more hours of daily screen time; possible sexual minority 1.58 (95% CI 0.92-2.24) more hours; also reports associations with problematic social media, video games, and mobile phone use (statistics cited in text and results)

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical analysis using multiple linear regression on cross-sectional ABCD Year 2 data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Annals of Epidemiology, 2023, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis (E7 rule); sexual orientation is demographic/sociodemographic predictor but paper includes cross-sectional design, which means FT_EC7 does not apply—keep INCLUDE.
notes: Sexual orientation is sociodemographic predictor; SM/phone variables are outcomes. Cross-sectional design precludes FT_EC7 application per E7.
